sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=32, col=29).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 87, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking sky blue 1x1 dots places magenta 2x2 squares at (row-1, col-1); clicking magenta squares moves them up-left by 1; clicking top bar small squares does nothing; clicking large yellow top bar square does nothing; clicking blue blob does nothing; clicking bottom white bar does nothing', 'ACTION7': "Listed as valid but produces 'Unknown action at index 1' error"}
- Object taxonomy: {'blue_blob': 'Blue 69-pixel object centered around (27,33), boundary rows 23-31, cols 29-37', 'magenta_squares': '2x2 magenta squares, moved up-left by clicking', 'top_bar_colors': '4 small squares: S(1,1), M(1,5), p(1,9), Y(1,13) + large 4x4 Y square at (3,30)', 'bottom_bar': 'White bar at row 63, 28 pixels wide'}
- HUD map: {'top_bar': 'rows 0-6 with colored indicators (S, M, p, Y small + large Y)', 'bottom_bar': 'white bar at row 63, likely HUD/timer'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Click all sky blue path dots in order (16 clicks)'}
- Level log: {'level1': '16 clicks to clear path dots', 'level2': '60+ clicks, 4 magenta squares remain, level not complete'}
- Cross-level notes: Level 2 has 8 sky blue dots that create magenta squares when clicked. 4 squares survived, 4 disappeared. Squares move up-left when clicked.
Current level:
- World model: 4 magenta squares on black board with blue blob center. Squares at approximately (32,13), (37,34), (53,44), (54,13).
- Goal model: Unknown. Squares cannot reach blob by clicking (up-left movement). Top bar, blob, and bottom bar clicks all do nothing.
- Recent findings: ['Bottom white bar is 28 pixels at row 63', 'Clicking bottom bar does nothing']
- Open questions: ['What completes level 2?', 'Why did 4 of 8 squares disappear?', 'Is there a pattern the squares need to form?']
- Plan: Try moving squares to overlap each other or to specific positions. Consider that maybe squares need to be moved off the board.
- Strategy log: ['tried clicking top bar colors then squares: no recoloring effect', 'tried clicking squares then top bar colors: no effect', 'tried clicking blue blob: no effect', 'tried clicking large yellow top bar square: no effect', 'tried clicking bottom white bar: no effect']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(1,13) then MOUSE(34,15): square moved but no recolor (step 76)', 'MOUSE(33,14) then MOUSE(1,13): square moved but no recolor (step 78)', 'MOUSE(27,33) clicking blob: no change (step 75)', 'MOUSE(4,31) large yellow square: no change (step 80)', 'MOUSE(63,32) bottom bar: no change (step 81)', 'ACTION7: Unknown action error', 'Moving squares repeatedly: no special effect observed']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 67 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have the current state:
- 5 magenta squares (including the one in the top bar at (1,5))
- 4 magenta squares on the board: (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13)
- Blue blob at rows 23-31, cols 29-37
- Top bar: S(1,1), M(1,5), p(1,9), Y(1,13) + large Y(3,30)
- Bottom white bar at row 63, 16 pixels (was 28 before, now 16)
Wait, the bottom white bar changed from 28 pixels to 16 pixels! That's significant. The bottom bar is shrinking, which suggests it's a timer or progress indicator.
Also, I notice the top bar has a magenta square at (1,5), which is one of the 5 magenta squares. So the top bar's M square is actually a magenta square that's part of the gameplay, not just a HUD indicator.
Let me reconsider the level:
- The bottom white bar is shrinking (28 -> 16 pixels). This suggests it's a timer or progress bar.
- The top bar has 4 colored squares: S, M, p, Y
- There are 4 magenta squares on the board
Maybe the goal is to move the squares to match the top bar colors? Or maybe the bottom bar shrinking indicates that I'm running out of time/steps?
Wait, the bottom bar was 28 pixels initially (after the level transformed), and now it's 16 pixels. That's a decrease of 12 pixels. I made 12 actions since the bar was last measured (from step 75 to step 87). So the bar decreases by 1 pixel per action? Let me check:
- Step 75: bar was 28 pixels
- Step 87: bar is 16 pixels
- Difference: 12 steps, 12 pixels decrease
So the bar decreases by 1 pixel per action. This means it's a timer/step counter.
If the bar reaches 0, the level might fail. So I need to complete the level before the bar reaches 0. Currently, the bar is 16 pixels, so I have about 16 actions left.
Given this constraint, I need to find the solution quickly. Let me think about what the goal might be:
1. The top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y
2. There are 4 magenta squares on the board
3. The squares move up-left when clicked
What if the goal is to move each square to a position that matches one of the top bar colors? For example:
- Move one square to the position of the S square (1,1)
- Move one square to the position of the M square (1,5)
- Move one square to the position of the p square (1,9)
- Move one square to the position of the Y square (1,13)
But the squares move up-left, so they can only reach positions where row and col both decrease. The squares are at:
- (31, 28): can reach (1, 28-30) = (1, -2), off the board
- (32, 13): can reach (1, 13-31) = (1, -18), off the board
- (53, 44): can reach (1, 44-52) = (1, -8), off the board
- (54, 13): can reach (1, 13-53) = (1, -40), off the board
None of the squares can reach the top bar positions without going off the board first.
Wait, let me reconsider. The squares move up-left by 1 when clicked. So after n clicks, a square at (r, c) moves to (r-n, c-n). For the square to reach row 1, it needs r-n = 1, so n = r-1. At that point, the column would be c - (r-1) = c - r + 1.
For each square:
- (31, 28): n = 30, col = 28 - 30 = -2 (off board)
- (32, 13): n = 31, col = 13 - 31 = -18 (off board)
- (53, 44): n = 52, col = 44 - 52 = -8 (off board)
- (54, 13): n = 53, col = 13 - 53 = -40 (off board)
So none of the squares can reach row 1 without going off the left edge first. This hypothesis doesn't work.
Let me think about this differently. The top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y. Maybe these correspond to the 4 squares, and I need to click each square a specific number of times based on the color?
Or maybe the goal is to click the top bar colors in a specific order, which then affects the squares?
Actually, let me reconsider the initial state. The initial state had:
- A diagonal gray path from the blue blob to the bottom-left
- Purple 3x3 squares at (4, 30) and (58, 3)
- A white shape at (52, 10)
After clicking the gray path dots, I got magenta squares. The path had 14 dots plus a 3x3 square. But I only got 4 magenta squares that remained.
Wait, I think I miscounted. Let me re-examine the initial state. The initial segmentation showed:
- 15 gray objects: 1 with 9 pixels (3x3 square) + 14 with 1 pixel each (dots)
- 2 purple objects: both 9 pixels (3x3 squares)
- 1 white object: 5 pixels (shape)
- 1 white object: 64 pixels (bar)
So the initial state had 15 gray objects (the path). When I clicked them, I created magenta squares. But only 4 squares remained. Where did the other 11 squares go?
Actually, I think the initial state had 8 sky blue dots (not gray dots). Let me re-examine...
Hmm, I'm confused about the initial state. Let me focus on the current state and try to find a solution.
Current state:
- 4 magenta squares: (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13)
- Blue blob at rows 23-31, cols 29-37
- Top bar: S(1,1), M(1,5), p(1,9), Y(1,13) + large Y(3,30)
- Bottom white bar: 16 pixels at row 63
The bottom bar is shrinking. I have about 16 actions left. Let me try a hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move all 4 squares to the same column or row.
Looking at the squares:
- (31, 28): row 31, col 28
- (32, 13): row 32, col 13
- (53, 44): row 53, col 44
- (54, 13): row 54, col 13
Notice that (32, 13) and (54, 13) have the same column (13). If I move (54, 13) up by 22 clicks, it would be at (32, -9), which is off the board. But if I move it 22 clicks, it would be at (32, -9), not (32, 13).
Wait, the squares move up-left diagonally, so they can't stay in the same column. Each click moves them up by 1 and left by 1.
Let me try a different hypothesis. What if the goal is to move the squares so they form a specific pattern, like a diagonal line or a rectangle?
For a diagonal line, all squares would need to be on the same diagonal. The diagonal is defined by row - col = constant.
- (31, 28): 31 - 28 = 3
- (32, 13): 32 - 13 = 19
- (53, 44): 53 - 44 = 9
- (54, 13): 54 - 13 = 41
The diagonals are different. When a square moves up-left by 1, row - col stays the same (since both decrease by 1). So the diagonal is invariant under the move operation.
This means the squares can never be on the same diagonal unless they start on the same diagonal. Since they don't, they can never form a diagonal line.
Let me try another hypothesis. What if the goal is to move the squares to specific target positions, and the targets are indicated by the top bar colors?
The top bar has:
- S (sky blue) at (1, 1)
- M (magenta) at (1, 5)
- p (purple) at (1, 9)
- Y (yellow) at (1, 13)
Maybe each square corresponds to one color, and I need to move the square to a position related to that color. But the squares are all magenta, so they all correspond to M?
Actually, wait. Let me look at the initial state again. The initial state had a gray path from the blue blob to the bottom-left. The path dots were clicked, creating magenta squares. The path had a specific direction (top-right to bottom-left), and the squares move in the opposite direction (up-left, which is top-left).
Hmm, actually up-left is not the opposite of the path direction. The path goes from top-right to bottom-left, which is down-left. The squares move up-left, which is up and left.
Let me think about this differently. The initial state had anchor points (purple squares, white shape) that might indicate target positions for the magenta squares. The purple squares were at (4, 30) and (58, 3), and the white shape was at (52, 10).
What if the goal is to move the magenta squares to these anchor positions? But the anchor objects are no longer visible, so I need to remember their positions.
For the square at (31, 28) to reach (4, 30):
- It needs to move up by 27 and right by 2. But the square only moves up-left, not up-right. So it can't reach (4, 30).
For the square at (32, 13) to reach (58, 3):
- It needs to move down by 26 and left by 10. But the square only moves up-left, not down-left. So it can't reach (58, 3).
This hypothesis doesn't work either.
OK, let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to click the squares in a specific order, and the order is determined by their positions?
Looking at the squares:
- (31, 28): closest to the blob
- (32, 13): left
- (53, 44): bottom-right
- (54, 13): bottom-left
What if I need to click them in the order: closest to blob first, then left, then bottom-right, then bottom-left?
Or maybe the order is based on the top bar colors: S, M, p, Y. The squares are all magenta (M), so maybe I need to click them in the order of their proximity to the M square in the top bar?
The M square is at (1, 5). The distances from each square to (1, 5):
- (31, 28): |31-1| + |28-5| = 30 + 23 = 53
- (32, 13): |32-1| + |13-5| = 31 + 8 = 39
- (53, 44): |53-1| + |44-5| = 52 + 39 = 91
- (54, 13): |54-1| + |13-5| = 53 + 8 = 61
So the order from closest to farthest: (32, 13), (31, 28), (54, 13), (53, 44).
Let me try clicking them in this order and see if anything happens.
Actually, I think I should focus on the shrinking bottom bar. It's currently 16 pixels, and it decreases by 1 per action. If it reaches 0, the level might fail. So I need to find the solution quickly.
Let me try a bold hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move all 4 squares to overlap with the blue blob. The blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37.
For each square to overlap with the blob:
- (31, 28): already overlaps at (31, 29)
- (32, 13): needs to move up by 1 and right by 16. Can't move right.
- (53, 44): needs to move up by 22 and left by 7. After 22 clicks: (31, 22), which is at row 31 (blob's bottom) but col 22 (left of blob's left edge 29). After 7 clicks: (46, 37), which is at row 46 (below blob) and col 37 (blob's right edge). So it can't overlap with the blob.
- (54, 13): needs to move up by 23 and right by 16. Can't move right.
So only the square at (31, 28) can overlap with the blob, and it already does. The other squares can't reach the blob because they're too far left.
Hmm, this hypothesis doesn't work either.
Let me try yet another idea. What if the goal is to move the squares until they form a 2x2 grid (all 4 squares adjacent to each other)?
For a 2x2 grid of 2x2 squares:
- Top-left square at (r, c)
- Top-right square at (r, c+2)
- Bottom-left square at (r+2, c)
- Bottom-right square at (r+2, c+2)
The squares move up-left, so their relative positions change. Let me see if any two squares can become adjacent.
For (31, 28) and (32, 13):
- After n clicks on (31, 28): (31-n, 28-n)
- After m clicks on (32, 13): (32-m, 13-m)
- For them to be adjacent: |31-n - (32-m)| <= 1 and |28-n - (13-m)| <= 1
- Simplifying: |m - n - 1| <= 1 and |15 - n + m| <= 1
- From the second: n - m = 14 or 15 or 16
- From the first: m - n = 0, 1, or 2, so n - m = 0, -1, or -2
- Contradiction: n - m can't be both 14-16 and 0 to -2
So these two squares can never be adjacent. This hypothesis doesn't work.
OK, I'm running out of ideas. Let me try something simple: click all 4 squares once each, in some order, and see if anything happens. Maybe the goal is to click all squares a certain number of times.
Actually, let me reconsider the bottom bar. It's shrinking from 28 to 16 pixels over 12 actions. If it reaches 0, the level might fail. But what if the level completes when the bar reaches a certain value?
The initial bar was 64 pixels (in the very first frame), and now it's 16 pixels. The level transformed after I clicked the gray path dots. Maybe the bar represents the number of remaining actions, and the level completes when the bar reaches 0?
If that's the case, I should just keep clicking until the bar reaches 0. But that doesn't make sense because the level would complete automatically without me doing anything specific.
Let me try a different hypothesis. What if the bottom bar represents the number of remaining gray path dots to click? Initially, there were 14 dots + 1 square = 15 objects. But the bar was 64 pixels, not 15. So this doesn't match.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial state. The initial bottom bar was 64 pixels (the full width of the board). After the level transformed, the bar was 28 pixels. Now it's 16 pixels.
The bar decreased from 64 to 28 after I clicked the gray path dots. That's a decrease of 36 pixels. I clicked about 15 gray objects (14 dots + 1 square). So each click decreased the bar by about 2.4 pixels? That doesn't make sense.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The initial state had a 64-pixel white bar at row 63. After clicking all the gray path dots, the bar became 28 pixels. Then after 12 more actions, it became 16 pixels.
The decrease from 64 to 28 happened when the level transformed (after clicking the gray dots). The decrease from 28 to 16 happened over 12 actions, which is about 1 pixel per action.
So the bar might represent the remaining actions or steps. If it reaches 0, the level might fail. Or maybe the bar represents something else, like the number of remaining squares to move.
There are 4 squares on the board. The bar is 16 pixels. If each square needs to be moved 4 times, that's 16 actions. So the bar might represent the total number of moves needed.
Let me try moving each square 4 times and see if the level completes.
Actually, I just realized something. The top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y. There are 4 squares. Maybe each color corresponds to a square, and I need to move each square a number of times equal to the color's position in the top bar?
The top bar positions:
- S at col 1
- M at col 5
- p at col 9
- Y at col 13
So I need to move the squares 1, 5, 9, and 13 times? That's 1 + 5 + 9 + 13 = 28 actions. But the bar is 16 pixels, not 28.
Hmm, that doesn't match. Let me try a different interpretation.
Actually, let me just try moving each square once and see if anything happens. If the bar decreases by 1 per action, and there are 4 squares, then clicking all 4 squares would decrease the bar by 4, from 16 to 12.
Let me try this and see if the level completes or if anything special happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key observation: bottom white bar shrinks from 28 to 16 pixels over 12 actions (~1/action)
# 4 magenta squares on board. Top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y
# Hypothesis: need to move each square to match top bar color positions
# Top bar colors at cols: S=1, M=5, p=9, Y=13
# Squares at: (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13)
# 
# New hypothesis: squares need to be moved to columns matching top bar colors
# Square at col 13 already matches Y's col 13
# Need to move other squares to cols 1, 5, 9
# But squares move diagonally (up-left), so col decreases with each click
# 
# Let me try: move each square once to see if bar changes differently
# Squares: (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13)

# Click all 4 squares once each
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 28},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 13},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 53, 'col': 44},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 54, 'col': 13}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking magenta squares moves them up-left by 1; bottom white bar shrinks ~1 pixel per action"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_blob": "Blue blob, rows 23-31, cols 29-37", "magenta_squares": "2x2 magenta squares that move up-left when clicked", "top_bar": "S(1,1), M(1,5), p(1,9), Y(1,13) + large Y(3,30)", "bottom_bar": "White bar at row 63, shrinking timer"}, "hud_map": {"top_bar": "rows 0-9 with colored indicators", "bottom_bar": "white bar at row 63, shrinks 1 pixel per action"}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Click all path dots in order"}, "level_log": {"level1": "16 clicks", "level2": "67 clicks, 4 magenta squares, bar=16px"}, "cross_level_notes": "Level 2: bottom bar shrinks as timer; 4 squares need to be positioned correctly"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "4 magenta squares at (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13); blue blob; top bar 4 colors; bottom bar=16px timer", "goal_model": "HYPOTHESIS: Move squares to positions matching top bar color columns (1,5,9,13)", "recent_findings": ["Bottom bar shrinks ~1px per action (28->16 in 12 steps)", "Square overlaps blob with no special effect"], "open_questions": ["What completes level 2 before bar reaches 0?"], "current_plan": "Click all 4 squares once to test if bar changes differently", "strategy_log": ["Moving square into blob: no effect"], "failed_probes": ["Top bar clicks", "Bottom bar click", "Blob click", "Square into blob overlap"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Cross-level notes: Level 2 has 8 sky blue dots that create magenta squares when clicked. 4 squares survived, 4 disappeared. Squares move up-left when clicked.
Current level:
- World model: 4 magenta squares on black board with blue blob center. Squares at approximately (32,13), (37,34), (53,44), (54,13).
- Goal model: Unknown. Squares cannot reach blob by clicking (up-left movement). Top bar, blob, and bottom bar clicks all do nothing.
- Recent findings: ['Bottom white bar is 28 pixels at row 63', 'Clicking bottom bar does nothing']
- Open questions: ['What completes level 2?', 'Why did 4 of 8 squares disappear?', 'Is there a pattern the squares need to form?']
- Plan: Try moving squares to overlap each other or to specific positions. Consider that maybe squares need to be moved off the board.
- Strategy log: ['tried clicking top bar colors then squares: no recoloring effect', 'tried clicking squares then top bar colors: no effect', 'tried clicking blue blob: no effect', 'tried clicking large yellow top bar square: no effect', 'tried clicking bottom white bar: no effect']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(1,13) then MOUSE(34,15): square moved but no recolor (step 76)', 'MOUSE(33,14) then MOUSE(1,13): square moved but no recolor (step 78)', 'MOUSE(27,33) clicking blob: no change (step 75)', 'MOUSE(4,31) large yellow square: no change (step 80)', 'MOUSE(63,32) bottom bar: no change (step 81)', 'ACTION7: Unknown action error', 'Moving squares repeatedly: no special effect observed']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 67 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have the current state:
- 5 magenta squares (including the one in the top bar at (1,5))
- 4 magenta squares on the board: (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13)
- Blue blob at rows 23-31, cols 29-37
- Top bar: S(1,1), M(1,5), p(1,9), Y(1,13) + large Y(3,30)
- Bottom white bar at row 63, 16 pixels (was 28 before, now 16)
Wait, the bottom white bar changed from 28 pixels to 16 pixels! That's significant. The bottom bar is shrinking, which suggests it's a timer or progress indicator.
Also, I notice the top bar has a magenta square at (1,5), which is one of the 5 magenta squares. So the top bar's M square is actually a magenta square that's part of the gameplay, not just a HUD indicator.
Let me reconsider the level:
- The bottom white bar is shrinking (28 -> 16 pixels). This suggests it's a timer or progress bar.
- The top bar has 4 colored squares: S, M, p, Y
- There are 4 magenta squares on the board
Maybe the goal is to move the squares to match the top bar colors? Or maybe the bottom bar shrinking indicates that I'm running out of time/steps?
Wait, the bottom bar was 28 pixels initially (after the level transformed), and now it's 16 pixels. That's a decrease of 12 pixels. I made 12 actions since the bar was last measured (from step 75 to step 87). So the bar decreases by 1 pixel per action? Let me check:
- Step 75: bar was 28 pixels
- Step 87: bar is 16 pixels
- Difference: 12 steps, 12 pixels decrease
So the bar decreases by 1 pixel per action. This means it's a timer/step counter.
If the bar reaches 0, the level might fail. So I need to complete the level before the bar reaches 0. Currently, the bar is 16 pixels, so I have about 16 actions left.
Given this constraint, I need to find the solution quickly. Let me think about what the goal might be:
1. The top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y
2. There are 4 magenta squares on the board
3. The squares move up-left when clicked
What if the goal is to move each square to a position that matches one of the top bar colors? For example:
- Move one square to the position of the S square (1,1)
- Move one square to the position of the M square (1,5)
- Move one square to the position of the p square (1,9)
- Move one square to the position of the Y square (1,13)
But the squares move up-left, so they can only reach positions where row and col both decrease. The squares are at:
- (31, 28): can reach (1, 28-30) = (1, -2), off the board
- (32, 13): can reach (1, 13-31) = (1, -18), off the board
- (53, 44): can reach (1, 44-52) = (1, -8), off the board
- (54, 13): can reach (1, 13-53) = (1, -40), off the board
None of the squares can reach the top bar positions without going off the board first.
Wait, let me reconsider. The squares move up-left by 1 when clicked. So after n clicks, a square at (r, c) moves to (r-n, c-n). For the square to reach row 1, it needs r-n = 1, so n = r-1. At that point, the column would be c - (r-1) = c - r + 1.
For each square:
- (31, 28): n = 30, col = 28 - 30 = -2 (off board)
- (32, 13): n = 31, col = 13 - 31 = -18 (off board)
- (53, 44): n = 52, col = 44 - 52 = -8 (off board)
- (54, 13): n = 53, col = 13 - 53 = -40 (off board)
So none of the squares can reach row 1 without going off the left edge first. This hypothesis doesn't work.
Let me think about this differently. The top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y. Maybe these correspond to the 4 squares, and I need to click each square a specific number of times based on the color?
Or maybe the goal is to click the top bar colors in a specific order, which then affects the squares?
Actually, let me reconsider the initial state. The initial state had:
- A diagonal gray path from the blue blob to the bottom-left
- Purple 3x3 squares at (4, 30) and (58, 3)
- A white shape at (52, 10)
After clicking the gray path dots, I got magenta squares. The path had 14 dots plus a 3x3 square. But I only got 4 magenta squares that remained.
Wait, I think I miscounted. Let me re-examine the initial state. The initial segmentation showed:
- 15 gray objects: 1 with 9 pixels (3x3 square) + 14 with 1 pixel each (dots)
- 2 purple objects: both 9 pixels (3x3 squares)
- 1 white object: 5 pixels (shape)
- 1 white object: 64 pixels (bar)
So the initial state had 15 gray objects (the path). When I clicked them, I created magenta squares. But only 4 squares remained. Where did the other 11 squares go?
Actually, I think the initial state had 8 sky blue dots (not gray dots). Let me re-examine...
Hmm, I'm confused about the initial state. Let me focus on the current state and try to find a solution.
Current state:
- 4 magenta squares: (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13)
- Blue blob at rows 23-31, cols 29-37
- Top bar: S(1,1), M(1,5), p(1,9), Y(1,13) + large Y(3,30)
- Bottom white bar: 16 pixels at row 63
The bottom bar is shrinking. I have about 16 actions left. Let me try a hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move all 4 squares to the same column or row.
Looking at the squares:
- (31, 28): row 31, col 28
- (32, 13): row 32, col 13
- (53, 44): row 53, col 44
- (54, 13): row 54, col 13
Notice that (32, 13) and (54, 13) have the same column (13). If I move (54, 13) up by 22 clicks, it would be at (32, -9), which is off the board. But if I move it 22 clicks, it would be at (32, -9), not (32, 13).
Wait, the squares move up-left diagonally, so they can't stay in the same column. Each click moves them up by 1 and left by 1.
Let me try a different hypothesis. What if the goal is to move the squares so they form a specific pattern, like a diagonal line or a rectangle?
For a diagonal line, all squares would need to be on the same diagonal. The diagonal is defined by row - col = constant.
- (31, 28): 31 - 28 = 3
- (32, 13): 32 - 13 = 19
- (53, 44): 53 - 44 = 9
- (54, 13): 54 - 13 = 41
The diagonals are different. When a square moves up-left by 1, row - col stays the same (since both decrease by 1). So the diagonal is invariant under the move operation.
This means the squares can never be on the same diagonal unless they start on the same diagonal. Since they don't, they can never form a diagonal line.
Let me try another hypothesis. What if the goal is to move the squares to specific target positions, and the targets are indicated by the top bar colors?
The top bar has:
- S (sky blue) at (1, 1)
- M (magenta) at (1, 5)
- p (purple) at (1, 9)
- Y (yellow) at (1, 13)
Maybe each square corresponds to one color, and I need to move the square to a position related to that color. But the squares are all magenta, so they all correspond to M?
Actually, wait. Let me look at the initial state again. The initial state had a gray path from the blue blob to the bottom-left. The path dots were clicked, creating magenta squares. The path had a specific direction (top-right to bottom-left), and the squares move in the opposite direction (up-left, which is top-left).
Hmm, actually up-left is not the opposite of the path direction. The path goes from top-right to bottom-left, which is down-left. The squares move up-left, which is up and left.
Let me think about this differently. The initial state had anchor points (purple squares, white shape) that might indicate target positions for the magenta squares. The purple squares were at (4, 30) and (58, 3), and the white shape was at (52, 10).
What if the goal is to move the magenta squares to these anchor positions? But the anchor objects are no longer visible, so I need to remember their positions.
For the square at (31, 28) to reach (4, 30):
- It needs to move up by 27 and right by 2. But the square only moves up-left, not up-right. So it can't reach (4, 30).
For the square at (32, 13) to reach (58, 3):
- It needs to move down by 26 and left by 10. But the square only moves up-left, not down-left. So it can't reach (58, 3).
This hypothesis doesn't work either.
OK, let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to click the squares in a specific order, and the order is determined by their positions?
Looking at the squares:
- (31, 28): closest to the blob
- (32, 13): left
- (53, 44): bottom-right
- (54, 13): bottom-left
What if I need to click them in the order: closest to blob first, then left, then bottom-right, then bottom-left?
Or maybe the order is based on the top bar colors: S, M, p, Y. The squares are all magenta (M), so maybe I need to click them in the order of their proximity to the M square in the top bar?
The M square is at (1, 5). The distances from each square to (1, 5):
- (31, 28): |31-1| + |28-5| = 30 + 23 = 53
- (32, 13): |32-1| + |13-5| = 31 + 8 = 39
- (53, 44): |53-1| + |44-5| = 52 + 39 = 91
- (54, 13): |54-1| + |13-5| = 53 + 8 = 61
So the order from closest to farthest: (32, 13), (31, 28), (54, 13), (53, 44).
Let me try clicking them in this order and see if anything happens.
Actually, I think I should focus on the shrinking bottom bar. It's currently 16 pixels, and it decreases by 1 per action. If it reaches 0, the level might fail. So I need to find the solution quickly.
Let me try a bold hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move all 4 squares to overlap with the blue blob. The blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37.
For each square to overlap with the blob:
- (31, 28): already overlaps at (31, 29)
- (32, 13): needs to move up by 1 and right by 16. Can't move right.
- (53, 44): needs to move up by 22 and left by 7. After 22 clicks: (31, 22), which is at row 31 (blob's bottom) but col 22 (left of blob's left edge 29). After 7 clicks: (46, 37), which is at row 46 (below blob) and col 37 (blob's right edge). So it can't overlap with the blob.
- (54, 13): needs to move up by 23 and right by 16. Can't move right.
So only the square at (31, 28) can overlap with the blob, and it already does. The other squares can't reach the blob because they're too far left.
Hmm, this hypothesis doesn't work either.
Let me try yet another idea. What if the goal is to move the squares until they form a 2x2 grid (all 4 squares adjacent to each other)?
For a 2x2 grid of 2x2 squares:
- Top-left square at (r, c)
- Top-right square at (r, c+2)
- Bottom-left square at (r+2, c)
- Bottom-right square at (r+2, c+2)
The squares move up-left, so their relative positions change. Let me see if any two squares can become adjacent.
For (31, 28) and (32, 13):
- After n clicks on (31, 28): (31-n, 28-n)
- After m clicks on (32, 13): (32-m, 13-m)
- For them to be adjacent: |31-n - (32-m)| <= 1 and |28-n - (13-m)| <= 1
- Simplifying: |m - n - 1| <= 1 and |15 - n + m| <= 1
- From the second: n - m = 14 or 15 or 16
- From the first: m - n = 0, 1, or 2, so n - m = 0, -1, or -2
- Contradiction: n - m can't be both 14-16 and 0 to -2
So these two squares can never be adjacent. This hypothesis doesn't work.
OK, I'm running out of ideas. Let me try something simple: click all 4 squares once each, in some order, and see if anything happens. Maybe the goal is to click all squares a certain number of times.
Actually, let me reconsider the bottom bar. It's shrinking from 28 to 16 pixels over 12 actions. If it reaches 0, the level might fail. But what if the level completes when the bar reaches a certain value?
The initial bar was 64 pixels (in the very first frame), and now it's 16 pixels. The level transformed after I clicked the gray path dots. Maybe the bar represents the number of remaining actions, and the level completes when the bar reaches 0?
If that's the case, I should just keep clicking until the bar reaches 0. But that doesn't make sense because the level would complete automatically without me doing anything specific.
Let me try a different hypothesis. What if the bottom bar represents the number of remaining gray path dots to click? Initially, there were 14 dots + 1 square = 15 objects. But the bar was 64 pixels, not 15. So this doesn't match.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial state. The initial bottom bar was 64 pixels (the full width of the board). After the level transformed, the bar was 28 pixels. Now it's 16 pixels.
The bar decreased from 64 to 28 after I clicked the gray path dots. That's a decrease of 36 pixels. I clicked about 15 gray objects (14 dots + 1 square). So each click decreased the bar by about 2.4 pixels? That doesn't make sense.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The initial state had a 64-pixel white bar at row 63. After clicking all the gray path dots, the bar became 28 pixels. Then after 12 more actions, it became 16 pixels.
The decrease from 64 to 28 happened when the level transformed (after clicking the gray dots). The decrease from 28 to 16 happened over 12 actions, which is about 1 pixel per action.
So the bar might represent the remaining actions or steps. If it reaches 0, the level might fail. Or maybe the bar represents something else, like the number of remaining squares to move.
There are 4 squares on the board. The bar is 16 pixels. If each square needs to be moved 4 times, that's 16 actions. So the bar might represent the total number of moves needed.
Let me try moving each square 4 times and see if the level completes.
Actually, I just realized something. The top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y. There are 4 squares. Maybe each color corresponds to a square, and I need to move each square a number of times equal to the color's position in the top bar?
The top bar positions:
- S at col 1
- M at col 5
- p at col 9
- Y at col 13
So I need to move the squares 1, 5, 9, and 13 times? That's 1 + 5 + 9 + 13 = 28 actions. But the bar is 16 pixels, not 28.
Hmm, that doesn't match. Let me try a different interpretation.
Actually, let me just try moving each square once and see if anything happens. If the bar decreases by 1 per action, and there are 4 squares, then clicking all 4 squares would decrease the bar by 4, from 16 to 12.
Let me try this and see if the level completes or if anything special happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key observation: bottom white bar shrinks from 28 to 16 pixels over 12 actions (~1/action)
# 4 magenta squares on board. Top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y
# Hypothesis: need to move each square to match top bar color positions
# Top bar colors at cols: S=1, M=5, p=9, Y=13
# Squares at: (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13)
# 
# New hypothesis: squares need to be moved to columns matching top bar colors
# Square at col 13 already matches Y's col 13
# Need to move other squares to cols 1, 5, 9
# But squares move diagonally (up-left), so col decreases with each click
# 
# Let me try: move each square once to see if bar changes differently
# Squares: (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13)

# Click all 4 squares once each
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 28},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 13},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 53, 'col': 44},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 54, 'col': 13}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking magenta squares moves them up-left by 1; bottom white bar shrinks ~1 pixel per action"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_blob": "Blue blob, rows 23-31, cols 29-37", "magenta_squares": "2x2 magenta squares that move up-left when clicked", "top_bar": "S(1,1), M(1,5), p(1,9), Y(1,13) + large Y(3,30)", "bottom_bar": "White bar at row 63, shrinking timer"}, "hud_map": {"top_bar": "rows 0-9 with colored indicators", "bottom_bar": "white bar at row 63, shrinks 1 pixel per action"}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Click all path dots in order"}, "level_log": {"level1": "16 clicks", "level2": "67 clicks, 4 magenta squares, bar=16px"}, "cross_level_notes": "Level 2: bottom bar shrinks as timer; 4 squares need to be positioned correctly"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "4 magenta squares at (31,28), (32,13), (53,44), (54,13); blue blob; top bar 4 colors; bottom bar=16px timer", "goal_model": "HYPOTHESIS: Move squares to positions matching top bar color columns (1,5,9,13)", "recent_findings": ["Bottom bar shrinks ~1px per action (28->16 in 12 steps)", "Square overlaps blob with no special effect"], "open_questions": ["What completes level 2 before bar reaches 0?"], "current_plan": "Click all 4 squares once to test if bar changes differently", "strategy_log": ["Moving square into blob: no effect"], "failed_probes": ["Top bar clicks", "Bottom bar click", "Blob click", "Square into blob overlap"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
